name: Buffalo Wild Wings
cat: Food & Beverage / Casual Dining Restaurant Chain

political.s: Buffalo Wild Wings operated a PAC that raised approximately $209,872 in the 2015–2016 election cycle and gave $170,500 to federal candidates in 2013–2014, with a historical lean toward Republican recipients. The PAC has been largely inactive in recent cycles, with no identified affiliates or significant lobbying reported in 2024. BWW is now a subsidiary of Roark Capital-owned Inspire Brands and no longer files independently with the FEC.

charity.s: The Buffalo Wild Wings Foundation has raised and donated over $30 million since 2013, primarily in partnership with Boys & Girls Clubs of America to fund youth sports programs. In 2024 alone, the Foundation pledged more than $3 million to youth initiatives, including $1 million to BGCA and $2 million to over 300 local nonprofits. Community giving is channeled through the 'Team Up for Kids' and 'Eat Wings, Raise Funds' programs.
charity.focus: Youth sports, Boys & Girls Clubs of America, local community nonprofits

environment.s: Buffalo Wild Wings scores 25/100 on DitchCarbon's sustainability index, above 60% of the hospitality industry but still weak in absolute terms. As a subsidiary of Inspire Brands, BWW has no publicly disclosed carbon emissions data or documented climate reduction targets of its own. The brand has made limited sustainability moves, including engaging Quest Resource for a waste and recycling program across 516 U.S. locations.

labor.s: Buffalo Wild Wings has faced multiple wage and hour lawsuits across several states, including allegations of failing to pay proper minimum wage and overtime to tipped workers in violation of the FLSA. An EEOC charge was filed alleging religious discrimination, and separate EEOC complaints documented sexual harassment by a general manager that went unaddressed by management. Employee satisfaction surveys on Glassdoor and Comparably rank BWW's compensation and benefits in the bottom 5–10% of similarly sized companies.

dei.s: Under parent company Inspire Brands, Buffalo Wild Wings publicly promotes a multi-pillar DEI program including employee resource groups such as Champions of Women Leaders, Pride at Inspire (LGBTQ+), Black Voices @ Inspire, Inspirate (Latino/Hispanic), and InspirAsians. However, the EEOC filed a religious discrimination lawsuit against a BWW corporate operator in 2024, representing a gap between stated policy and practice. Overall, DEI commitments are relatively robust at the corporate level.

animals.s: Buffalo Wild Wings is a restaurant chain that serves chicken wings and other animal-based food products; it is not a consumer goods or cosmetics company involved in animal testing for products. The company does not have an animal testing policy as it is not applicable to its business model. Parent Inspire Brands states a commitment to humane animal treatment in its supply chain.

guns.s: Buffalo Wild Wings has maintained a corporate no-firearms policy at its company-owned restaurants since at least 2009, stating it has elected to exercise its right to restrict the carrying of firearms in its locations for the comfort and safety of guests. The policy applies to customer carry but exempts credentialed law enforcement officers. Enforcement varies by location and franchisee.

privacy.s: Buffalo Wild Wings collects a wide range of personal data including contact information, payment details, precise location, demographic information, and site usage data through its apps, websites, and loyalty program (Blazin' Rewards). The company shares consumer data with affiliated Inspire Brands entities and third-party vendors, and acknowledges selling or sharing personal information for targeted advertising — with opt-out rights offered in applicable states. The breadth of data collection and cross-brand sharing presents moderate privacy concerns.

execPay.s: Buffalo Wild Wings was taken private via acquisition by Inspire Brands in 2018, so no current SEC proxy disclosures or CEO pay ratio filings are publicly available. Historical pre-acquisition data (Comparably) shows top BWW executives earning up to $1.5M+, while rank-and-file restaurant workers earn $16–$22/hour. Employee compensation is rated in the bottom 10% of similarly sized companies by Comparably respondents, suggesting a significant gap between executive and frontline pay.

sec.note: Pay ratio extracted from proxy filing — verify manually for accuracy
